title: The manager who runs your life through perfectionism
author: Kalesh

The voice that never rests 

 There's a voice in your head that's been running things for a long time. Maybe since you were small. It's the one that tells...
htmlContent:
The voice that never rests

There's a voice in your head that's been running things for a long time. Maybe since you were small. It's the one that tells you nothing you do is good enough. The one that keeps a running list of every mistake you've ever made. The one that says if you just try harder, plan better, be more careful, you'll finally be safe.

I know this voice. I've hosted it for decades. It doesn't shout, exactly. It's more like a low hum that never stops. A manager who works the night shift and the day shift and every shift in between. No overtime pay. No vacation. No sick days.

Here's the thing. This manager isn't lazy. It's not cruel for the sake of being cruel. It's terrified. Deep down, it believes that if it stops managing, everything will fall apart. You'll get rejected. You'll fail publicly. You'll be abandoned. The worst thing that could happen will happen, and it will be your fault for not being perfect enough.

Richard Schwartz, the creator of Internal Family Systems therapy, calls this part the "manager." He describes it as a protector part that tries to keep you safe by controlling your environment, your behavior, and your emotions. It's not the enemy. It's a scared kid who took on a job no kid should have to do.

I see this part in clients all the time. It shows up as the person who rewrites an email five times before sending it. The one who can't leave a party without scanning the room for someone they might have offended. The one who spends two hours cleaning the kitchen after cooking a meal because any mess is a sign of failure.

Sound familiar? Stay with me here.

Where the manager was born

Perfectionism doesn't come from nowhere. It's not a personality trait you were born with. It's a survival strategy that your nervous system cooked up in response to a world that didn't feel safe.

For me, it started in a house where love was conditional. If I got good grades, I got attention. If I kept my room clean, I got approval. If I made a mistake, I got silence or criticism. So I learned to manage. I learned to anticipate every possible error before it happened. I learned to hold myself to standards that no human could meet.

Bessel van der Kolk, author of "The Body Keeps the Score," has spent decades studying how trauma shapes the brain and body. He talks about how children who grow up in unpredictable or emotionally neglectful environments develop hypervigilance as a coping mechanism. You're always scanning for danger. Always trying to control the uncontrollable.

The manager part is that hypervigilance turned inward. Instead of watching for threats in the outside world, you watch for threats inside yourself. A mistake. A flaw. A moment of imperfection that might prove you're not good enough.

Think about it. When was the last time your manager part kicked in? Maybe it was this morning, when you looked in the mirror and found something wrong with your appearance. Maybe it was at work, when you re-read a sentence ten times because you were afraid it wasn't perfect. Maybe it was right now, as you're reading this, and a voice is telling you that you should be doing something more productive.

That voice. That's the manager.

The body tax of constant management

Here's what no one tells you about perfectionism. It's exhausting. Not just mentally. Physically. Your body is paying the price for all that managing.

When your manager part is running the show, your nervous system stays in a state of low-grade threat response. Your cortisol levels stay elevated. Your muscles stay tense. Your digestion slows down. Your sleep gets shallow. You're running a marathon every single day, but you never cross the finish line.

I remember a period in my late twenties when I was managing everything. My job. My relationships. My appearance. My apartment. I had spreadsheets for my grocery budget. I had color-coded calendars. I had a morning routine that took two hours and left no room for spontaneity.

And I was miserable. My body was a knot of tension. I had headaches every afternoon. I couldn't remember the last time I'd laughed without checking myself. I was so busy managing my life that I wasn't actually living it.

Janina Fisher, a trauma specialist who writes extensively about structural dissociation, describes how our parts can take over our bodies. The manager part doesn't just live in your thoughts. It lives in your shoulders, your jaw, your stomach. It shows up as chronic pain, as tension headaches, as that tightness in your chest that never quite goes away.

Wild, right? Your perfectionism isn't just in your head. It's in your cells.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). It helps explain why so many of us developed these manager parts in the first place. We were trying to get love from people who couldn't give it, and we decided the problem was us.

What happens when you fire the manager

I need to be honest with you. This part is not going to be a neat, tidy conclusion. Because the manager doesn't just leave when you tell it to. It's been running your life for years. Decades. It doesn't know how to stop.

When I first started working with my own manager part, I tried to fire it. I told it I didn't need it anymore. I said I was done with perfectionism. I was going to be free and spontaneous and messy.

And you know what happened? Everything got worse. I missed deadlines. I forgot appointments. I ate junk food for three days straight. I felt like a teenager who'd been given the keys to the car without ever learning to drive. The manager had been holding the wheel, and without it, I crashed.

Here's what I learned. You don't fire the manager. You promote it. You give it a new job.

Instead of being the CEO of your life, it can be the receptionist. It can answer the phone when it rings, but it doesn't have to make all the decisions. It can remind you of your schedule, but it doesn't have to enforce it with an iron fist. It can help you organize your desk without organizing your entire soul.

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link). It gets into the science of how these parts form and how to work with them instead of against them.

Notice what just happened in your body when I said you don't have to fire the manager. Did something relax? Or did it tighten up, afraid that if you stop managing, everything will fall apart?

Both reactions are valid. Both are parts. The one that relaxes is the part that's tired of working so hard. The one that tightens is the manager itself, scared of losing its job.

Practical steps for working with the manager

I'm not going to give you a five-step plan to cure perfectionism forever. That's not how this works. But I can share what's helped me and the people I work with.

First, notice the manager without judgment. When you catch yourself rewriting that email for the fourth time, pause. Say to yourself, "Oh, there's the manager. It's trying to keep me safe." Don't fight it. Just notice it.

Second, ask the manager what it's afraid of. Not what it's afraid will happen if you send the imperfect email. What it's afraid will happen to you. To your sense of safety. To your place in the world. The manager is protecting something. Usually it's a younger part that was hurt before.

Third, thank the manager for its work. I know this sounds ridiculous. But that part has been working 24/7 for years to keep you safe. It deserves some gratitude. You can say it out loud or in your head. "Thank you for trying to protect me. I know you're scared. I'm here now."

Fourth, experiment with small imperfections. Send the email with one typo. Leave one dish in the sink overnight. Wear an outfit that's comfortable instead of perfect. See what happens. The world probably won't end. And if it does, you'll know the manager was right all along.

A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link). Boundaries are actually a way to let the manager relax. When you have clear limits, you don't need to manage every interaction. You just need to hold your boundary.

For understanding emotional neglect specifically, a helpful resource is Running on Empty (paid link). It explains how growing up without emotional attunement creates that inner manager who tries to compensate by controlling everything.

I'm not going to tell you that you can get rid of your manager part entirely. Maybe you can't. Maybe you don't need to. What I can tell you is that you can learn to live with it differently. You can stop letting it run your life. You can give it a smaller job. You can let it rest sometimes.

And in those moments when you send the imperfect email, leave the messy kitchen, wear the comfortable clothes, and the world doesn't end, you'll start to feel something new. Something that feels like freedom. Or maybe just relief.

Both are worth fighting for.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Running on Empty (paid link)
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link)
  • Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link)
  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link)
